items.prompt_md: Why must the loop condition check **both** `fast` and `fast.next` (not just `fast`)?
options: Because `fast.next.next` would fail on a `None` node when the list has an even number of nodes
To make the slow pointer move faster
It's only needed when the list is empty
Because Python requires two conditions in every `while` loop
items.answer: Because `fast.next.next` would fail on a `None` node when the list has an even number of nodes
items.explanation_md: `fast` advances two nodes per iteration. If `fast.next` were `None`, evaluating `fast.next.next` would raise — checking both guards every double-step.

items.prompt_md: You need the middle node of a singly linked list in a single traversal, without knowing its length beforehand. Which approach fits?
options: Count the length first, then do a second pass to node `n // 2`
Copy every value into an array and index into the middle
Move two pointers from the head — one node at a time, the other two nodes at a time — until the fast one runs out
Reverse the list, then take the head of the reversed list
items.answer: Move two pointers from the head — one node at a time, the other two nodes at a time — until the fast one runs out
items.explanation_md: Two pointers moving at different speeds land exactly on the midpoint in one pass — no length count and no O(n) extra storage.

items.prompt_md: What is the time and space complexity of the fast/slow pointer sweep to find the middle node?
options: O(log n) time, O(1) extra space
items.explanation_md: `fast` sweeps the whole list once (about n/2 loop iterations), and only two pointer variables are kept regardless of list length — O(n) time, O(1) extra space.
